OURRENT AFFAIRS.

The excursion of the Suffers into Derry is but one
more piece of evidence of the political fatuity of these
people. They saw a great chance—as they thought—

. to be important. In such a close fight as Derry was
boundto be: every individual vote was important, and
any organisation that might have the influencing of
‘even half-a-dozen votes became for the mioment of
yast importance. It gave an opportunity to the ‘‘ non-
poli{icn and non-sectarian ”’ Suffers in this country,

< and they did their little trick of proclaiming war on

“the Home Rule candidate. They snarled and showed

- their- teeth, but in the result they only proved that
' they had no power to bite. . Anyone of common sense
" would know that in & fight such as the fight at Derry
the. issue was so knit that nnﬁeoomlnry matter like the'

Sufiragette business would influence a single vote.
As Mr. O’Doherty, the Nationalist conducting agent,
said: ‘“If all the Suffragettes in the world came to
Derry they would not change a vote.” In (:he_freak
organ of the Suffers in Ireland we are informed, in @he
course of an article written by one of * Lhe.besxegmg
party ’ in Derry, that—‘‘ For the first time in Ireland
a definite anti-Government policy has been adopted
by Suffragists and a vigorous campaign is being carried
on.” The result of this ‘‘ vigorous ' little trick is
that the ‘‘ Government ™ candidatc reversed previous
decisions and got in by a majority of 57—that shows
what a lot of influence the Suffers exerted in Derry!
By the way, when does Skeffy intend to go to jail—the
event is rather overdue.

The ponderous very small fry of serious politicians
are amusing when one has time to give them a pass-
ing thought. With what fire, and evidence of convic-
tion, the great little Liberal in England consigns the
big Tory to political perdition, and with what hysterics
the great little Tory denounces Asquith and pours
scorn on Lloyd George! We are often amused at the
little great men, though for many years past we-are
too pre-occupied with other matters to spare time to
laugh at the great little ones. The Unionist women
of Kingstown had a meeting—or a meeting concerning
them was held—in that non-industrial but grimy
township. One B. N. Blood—a sniff of civil war
and wooden rifles even about the name—presided.
The report of the meeting is headed in Arnott’s paper,
““ Unionist Women of Kingstown,”” but the ‘speakers
appear to have been mostly men. A Mr. E. V.
Longworth: spoke—we hope this is not a misprint in -
the report for Miss E. V. Longworth, for even in these
days of window breaking we make distinctions. This
Longworth person, whoever he may be, is reported
partly thus: ‘‘ Continuing, the speaker said the Fran-
chise Bill had had to be dropped, and there never was
a more humiliating position than that in which Mr.
Asquith found himself to-day.”’ Thal is where the
humour comes in. Picture this Longworth chap, who-
ever he may be—as we have said we hope he is not
a printer’s error for a lady—picture this small fry at a
Kingstown meeting of alleged female Unionists—pro-
bably mostly composed of servant gitls pleasing their
mistresses for fear they might lose their places—paint
ing up Asquith in a superlatively humiliating light\
This suggests the smart five-shillings-a-week insurance
office clerk who sees how foolish not only the manager,

. but all the directors of his company are in connection

with the conduct of the business! Asquith should
retire to a cave now that Mr. (or Miss) K. V. Long-
worth has declared that he, Asquith, is in a most
humiliating position. -

The Land Commission like the Board of Works and
the Valuation Office is another nice little nest for the
“Saved.”  There are two Judicial Coinmissioners,
Titzgerald and Wylie, with the princely salaries of
£3,600 each. There is a half-and-half* arrangement
here, for one is ‘‘ Saved.’’ and one an ‘‘ Idolater.”
Of the four Commissioners two have £2,50C each and .
two £2,000 each. The only *“ Idolaler * on this perch
is Lynch, with £2,500. Wrench, Bailic and Stewart
are all ‘“ Saved.”” Wrench has £2,500 and the other
pair £2,000 each. - These salaries make one gasp.
Here are six rather ordinary  officials consuming an
aggrogate salary of £16,000 a year in this * poor-dis-
tressful country.”  The Secretary, Mrv. Beard, is
““ Saved *’ and gets £1,200 a year. There are two

A )}

$33

assistant secretaries, Messts. Green and Diennan, both
* Saved,” and each drawing £800 a year. The Ac-
eountant is an * Idolater’’ on £900, and the first
assistant accountant is one of the * Saved "’ on £550.
There ave two chief inspectors, one Mr. Wilson,
* Saved,” £1,000 a year, and one Mr. Hogan, an
““Idolater,”” £1,000. Of the thirty-five inspectors,
with an average salary, so far as we can hear, some-
thing like £800, twenty-five are ‘ Saved "’ and only
fen ** Idolaters.” When we come to the comparatively
Jow shelf of assistant inspectors at £400 a year we find
ihat out of sixteen, twelve are *‘ Idolaters '’ and four
* Saved. ™

In the intern staff Mr. Crookshank is ““ Saved’’ on
£1,000 a year. The legal advisor, £1,000, and two
assistants, £400 each, are all “ Saved.”’ There are
eighteen Examiners of Titles at £800 each and twelve
are ‘‘ Saved ”’ and six ‘‘ Idolaters *’; and of five Con-
veyance clerks at about £350, three are ‘ Saved’’ and
iwo ‘‘ Idolaters.”’

In the Collection of Annuities Department Messrs.
Crosby and Wolsley are both *“ Saved "’ and draw £600
and £500 respectively. The Registrar, Mr. Pollock,
£600, is *“ Saved,”’ but the two assistants are ‘‘ Idola-
iers.” The Head of Estates is a ‘‘ Saved '’ gentleman
formerly in Dunraven’s agent’s office, and he receives
£400 a year. The Head of Agreements is also
* Saved ”” on £500, but the Keeper of Records is an
** Idolater,”’ and so is the head of Vesting Department.
So far-as we have gone we calculate that in the
agaregate the “ Saved ” draw £51,600 a year, whilst
the ‘“Idolaters "—and there would be no Land Com-
wmission only for the great ‘ Idolaters’”” Land War—
only receive £28,250.

There are a few more of the positions that we might
set out, but we have perhaps given enough for the
present. Next week it is probable that we may cast
uurﬂscruﬁnizing eye over the Local Government Board
staff.

A Ballaghaderreen correspondent writes:—'* The
2 police of Ballaghaderreen are very careful that the citi-
zens of the town do not express their joy and admira-
tion on hearing good news by lighting bonfires on the
streets. Quite recently a well-known D.C. was fined
with costs for lighting' a fire on the street signalizing
the return of a mosft popular townsman from his honey-
moon. On last Friday night the police were cleverly
foiled. The electric: lights are generally switched on
at 8 p.m., but on Friday evening the streets were left
in total darkness till a majestic blaze burst forth from
a Iarge bonfire erected in front of the residence of Mr.
John Dillon, M.P. Then as if by magic other fires
showed themselves, and all at once the electric lamps
shone brilliantly through the town. The fires weve
quickly surrounded and scrutinized by the ‘ protectors
of the peace;’ but as the material for the ‘blaze’ was

of 2 highly inflammable nature, the flames were rather "

tao furious to be extinguished by a kick of a policeman’s
foot, so they were compelled to watch and wait till the
eonflagration in honour of Derry’s choice died. of its-own
aceord. Of course the police were easily hoodwinked
in the. darkness which prevailed that night. It would
be well to know if other business more serious than
Hghting fires on the streets is carried on in Ballagha-
derreen without hindrance from the R.[.C. More
anon.”” 3 - \

We firmly believe that many citizens in _Dul)lin and
other pl:\ceé go to great trouble to have their trumpery
little -hospitalities reported—and some o[“Lhcx:n are
trumpery and no mistalke. Murphy’s “ Ha penny
Dreadful ”’ runs a column of what they call * Bi-
weekly Jottings, by Our Social Editor,” but which one
of our humourists once parodied as  Our Social Refuse.
Vile Weekly Rottings (by Our Sacialist Editor).””

Let us take no more remote a date than January
31st last, and we see a column and a quarter of vile
weekly rottings, or, as Murphy’s paper chooses to call
them, “ Bi-weekly Jottings.”’ Here we find ““ jottings”*
of a dance in connection with a corporation styled «“ The
Templeogue Hockey and Tennis Clubs.”” We are told
that—

Mus. , one of the presidents, wore a grace-
ful gown of royal blue crepe de chene and satin
draped gold-spangled neti; Mrs. also a presi-
dent, wore a black satin toilette with gold lace
corsage swathed cerise velvet; Miss — -, the hon.
secretary, wore emerald green satin, veiled with
black and gold net. These hostesses were assisted
by Mr. and Mr. , hon. secs., and
all wore the badges of the Club in primrose and
chocolate colowrs.

How sweetly pretty and interesting to the public it
all is, to be sure. We wonder was the ‘ vile jotter 2™
specially invited, or did she or he get in under disguise
and take notes at the peril of being ‘‘ chucked out ”?
The jottings go on to describe the dress of the Lady

described, all of course as a matter “of public ine

boys and not between the y and s. It i somethi
connected with a place called St. Andrew’s College,:
wherever that may be. Quite a large number of dresses

is yet another dose, **in the public interest,”” of the

Of course, all this sort of thing is sickening, and it
is trailing journalism at a very low level. But only a
very: simple person would imagine that this reporting
had to be done at the risk of being chucked out and
ducked in a pond if the ‘* jotter ' were discovered. It
is all part of the shoddy genteel rush for publicity pan-
dered to by cateh ha’penny and cateh penny journalism.

The members of ‘“ the trade ™ in Dundalk are per-
turbed over the proposed closing of their business pre-
mises on St. Patrick’s Day. T rs that since the
advent of the Gaelic League the National Festival has
been celebrated in proper spirit in Dun Dealean. Tor
ten years or so all the local business premises have
been closed on that day. During the past week a
deputation from the local branch of the Gaclic League,
consisting of the Administrator, Rev. P. Lyons; Rev.
M. Kerr; C.C., and Rev. H. Tohall, C.C., waited on
the Urban Council to enlist their sympathy regarding
the movement for the change of the weekly market
from the 17th March to the following day as was done
on a previous occasion in connection with the monthly
fair. The result is that the local bungs are up in
arms against the proposal. They leld a caucus meet-
ing recently to which injured brethren of different call-
i were invited. The meeting was called by Mr. J. M.
Johnson, who is secretary of the local Bung Association.
The chair was occupied by a Mr. Hamill (a quondam
Gaelic Leaguer), who is also in the Bung line. The
attendance was miserably small, and included Messrs.
Craig, Thompson, Tempest and Murray. These gentle-
men, although they oppose the movement led by the
Administrator of the Parish and the local clergy, do not
object to taking Catholic money. Dundalk is essentially
a Catholic town, and the townspeople (with the excep-
tion of a handful of bungs) are desirious of having the
National Holiday properly observed. The only gentle-
man to vote in opposition to this brazen performance
at the caucus we have referred to was a Catholic hard-
ware man. We trust that the Catholics of North
Louth will not brook this business Next week we
may return to the subject.

““ The third grand annual entertainment by the pupils
of the girls’ national school’’ in Tallow, Co. Waterford,
has been held. There was a humorous song, ‘‘ Molly
O'Halloran,”” and a comic recitation, ‘ The Little
Black Coon.”  So far as the lengthened report of the
proceedings in a local paper goes, there was not as
much as one item in the Irish language, but the pro-
ceedings were brought to a close by the singing of
*“ Nation Once Again!”’ We believe there is a branch
of the Gaelic League in Tallow.

the operation of the law under Home Rule. In view
of all the talk about safeguards, it is worth noting that
the Free and Accepted have taken care fo protect their
secret society. ) n t

Mr. Hugh T. Barric, M.P., took the Derry beating
badly.  Addressing a crowd in Derry after the election,
he said :—** Fortune has temporarily deserted us in the
battle of to-day. The lesson of this fight—and I hope
it will be taken to heart—is that the breed of Lundg
is not yet extinct’’ (groans). Certainly the Carrion
Crows and their supporters got a bad knock in Derry.

We suppose Derry has given Arnobt’s paper a fit of
indecision—after all the cat may gently jump on the
Home Rule side after all. In Tuesday’s issue the
leading article is on ‘‘ Iimigration and Patriotism.”
The article is a sort of a reply to a correspondent who is
considering the question—to emigrate or not to em

grate. Notwithstanding the Budget and the possibi
lity of ** civil war,”” and the North-Fast Ulster ‘* fact,”
Arnott’s ** Times’” coolly say ““In the first place,

we suggest to * C. I.. T.” that he might find himself
no better oft in any of the Dominions, or in any foreign
country, than he now finds himself in Ireland.”” Agaie
the wily ““Alf,”” that was but yesterday puffing like
Falstaff about the Budget, says :—

Does
such fac

C. L. T.” expect to find in foreign parfs
s for the type of education which he
prizes ? As for his taxes, he would
pay less than £30 to any other Government.
The same remark applies to local taxes; and there
are worse roads in Canada and Australia than-even
in rural Treland. We admit that the insurance
tax is oppressive and objectionable, but a £10 griev-
ance ought not to drive any Irishman to emigra-
tion. ““C. L. T.” complains that the employee
dominates the employer in Ireland. A very slight
study of the labour legislation of Canada, fhe
United States, and Ausbralia will show him thas
he may easily go farther and fare worse.

And whilst the wooden guns are fresh in our memo-
ries and ** Dolly’s Brae” is still in our ears, “Alf ”* says
*‘ There is a splendid future before Ireland as an agri-
cultural country. In a few years we should
not only be able to export vast quantities of food, but
to feed ourselves cheaply and well. It seems to ns,
then, that, in regard to the necessaries, comforts and
amenities of life, our correspondent’s case for emigra-
tion cannot stand.”” And then “Alf,” forgetting about
the Budget and wooden rifles, forgetting that the ad-
jective ““grim* is in the dictionary, soars like a flying
machine and declares :—

That is the practical side of the question, buk
surely we may appeal to ““ C L. T.”” on a higher
ground. We believe that Ireland is on the eve of
a period not merely of material, but of social an@
intellectual, development. For hundreds of years
the two great classes in Ireland have ‘been
estranged, and have suffered much at each other’s

hands. In epite of sectarian troubles and political
quarrels.they are gradually coming together. The
present exclusion of the class which ** C. L. T.
represents from the offices of local government is,
we are confident, only ‘a temporary condition. The
social and political gulf which has divided Ireland
for four centuries cannot be bridged in a day; but
every faithful observer of the times knows that the
gap is closing.

What does all this signify ? Has Arnott’s ** Times”
given up the losing game of the Carrion Crows? Pro-
bably it is only a passing chill brought on by the cold
wind from Derry. .

Mr. John \W. O'Ryan, 754 Tremont Street, Boston,
Mass., U.S.A., in the course of « letter just to hand,
says :—'“ Reading your admirable comments upon the
police testimonials engineered by Bung, and also taking
notes of the safeguards’ so abundantly provided ‘ on
the floor of the House,” one is moved to speculate
whether the national administration which is profess-
edly being constituted at the present time, will hava
the inherent power to enact an ordinance summarily
discharging any policeman who accepts a testimonial,
which, to the impartial observer, looks too much like a
bribe—ea post facto. The insensate and unredeemed
flunkeyism which you are handling so caustically makes
self-respecting Trish Nationalists blush for the past
glories of holy Ireland, while they thank God for a
LEADER to treat them as they deserve.”

The Departmental dinner of the bigotted old Board
of Works came off on Saturday night. Last week we
suggested that in due time glowing reports of the affair
would appear in the Dublin dailies. But our notes last
week rather upset the old bigotted Board, and we
smiled when we saw that the report of the affair in the
‘“ Trish Times’’ only spun out to the extent of 26 lines!
Of the nominated positions in this den of bigotry 59 are
held by the ‘ Saved,”” who draw £24,080 a year, whilst
the “ Idolaters’” have only six positions and only draw
£2,100. The ‘“Irish Times’’ report of the dinner
says :—

The above function took place in the Aberdeen
Hall, Gresham Hotel, on Saturday evening. The
Chairman of the Board, Sir George Holmes,
K.C.B., K.C.V.0., presided. Many guests invited
by members of the Board and of the staff were
present from other departments. The Chairman,
in proposing the toast of *‘ The Guests,’ which was
responded to by Mr. T. P. Gill, referred to the
pleasant relations between the Board and other
departments. The toast of ‘“ The Board of Works”’
was proposed by the Right Hon. Sir Henry Robin-
son, K.C.B., Vice-President of the Local Govern-
ment Board, and responded to by Mr. Commis-
sioner Hanson. ‘‘ The Health of the Chairman *
was proposed by Mr. Joseph A. Glynn, Chairman,
National Health Insurance Commission.

So the old bigotted Board succeeded in drawing two
of our very few leading ‘“‘Idolatrous’’ officials to boost
their bigotted Board. The old crowd worked the thing
well. ~ They would be a long time before they nomi-
nated Mr. T. P. Gill or Mr. J. A. Glynn to a fat salary
amongst their clique, but no doubt they were very proud
in securing them to grace the'dinner of this bigotted
Board. We suppose there were far more guests than
members of the Board of Works staff at the dinner.

a Jolly Good Fellow'” to Boss Holmes and Secretary

In the course of a note from a distinguished clergy-
man received this morning, he asks us :—** By the bye,
have you seen the report of last night’s doings at Bel-
vedere College—two plays in FRENCH I As a matter
of fact we had not noticed it, but we made it our busi-
ness to look it up. The *‘ Freeman,” of which M.
Minch is one of the directors, had half a column about
it.  We are told the theatrical programme comprised
an amusing little comedy, ** La Grammaire,”” by Eu- °
geue Labiche, which afforded adequate scope for a dis-
play of juvenile talent, and a dainty sketch by the same
author, entitled, *“ Les Deux Tirnides. "’ The former
presents a cleverly wrought out picture of a self-con-
scious merchant retired from business cares, who sees
bright hopes of his becoming a man of public note in
his own little world, at le so long as his daughter
remains with him to help him in his letter-writing. and
save him from the pitfalls which his ignorance of gram-
mar exposes him to. We are rather left in doubt as to

e THE VIGILANCE CAMPAIGN.
N HE war on dirty papers ought to proceed with
venewed vigour just now.. Various Lenten
Pastorals in Ireland, probably all of them, give promi-
nence to the fight for newspaper cleanliness and the
matter is thus prominently brought again before the
attention of the whole country.
The Limerick Vigilance Committee, as they werve the
first to make a public holocaust of dirty papers, were
-also the first in starting the Good Literature Barrow.
That Barrow is now an established institution in
Limerick, and in connection with it the Vigilance Com-
mittee are inaugurating a prize scheme by which the
purchasers of papers and books at their Good Literature
Barrow will be entitled to compete. Ior each penny
spent at the Barrow a coupon will be given. This will
entitle the holder to participate in a weekly draw for
three prizes, viz.—Ist prize, in cash; 2nd prize,
books or papers to value Is.; 3rd prize, papers or books
to value 6d. The coupons issued one Sunday will be
drawn for and the result announced on the Barrow the
following Sunday, when holders of the winning coupons
may claim their prizes on producing the winning num-
ber. The Barrow will be stationed on Sunday oufside
the Augustinian Church from 9 o’clock to 11.15, and
‘near the Franciscan Church from 11.20 to 2 o’clock.
No doubt this schéme will give a great fillip to the
business of the Barrow, and we hope that other centres
will not be slow to follow theé lead. of Limerick: We
haveseen that the individual energy of one humble but
carnest man in Queenstown has resulted in the estab-
lishment of a Barrow in that important centre. We

. hear that-even the Waterford Vigilance Commiftee,

concerning the inactivity of which there were references
in one of our recent issues, has come to life again. A
meeting was held last week, and we are glad to hear
that we may expect activity there in a short time.
We have not heard that Kilkenny has stirred ifself;
and we have yet to learn that any move has been made
in Thurles. :

In Dublin considerable worlk is being, and has been,
done, and the prosecution of two young men \Yho, with
others, were distributing Vigilance leaflets, is one of

“the best things that could have happened; and the

ON BRAGGING AND STAGNATION.

OINCIDENCES are sometimes both curious and
illuminating. A week or ‘two ago, an Irishman
residing in South Africa published an indignant com-
plaint in the LEADER with regard to an Tiish firm of
sack-makers who had refused a large order, on the
rather astonishing ground that *“ we do not make goods
for export.’”” A strange refusal, indeed, based upon
an extraordinary (not to say erratic) reason.  Upon
which the correspondent commented in these caustic
terms: ‘‘ So long as manufacturers of this stamp refuse
to take advantage of good business outside their own
country, then your effort to stem the tide of emigra-
tion will be fruitless; and while they refuse to manu-
facture for export, our unfortunate country will go
on exporting the best bone and sinew of the land to
help to enrich manufacturers in other lands.”’
By an interesting coincidence, the same issue of this
journal contained a’wild and whirling defence of similar
methods on the part of the Irish C.T.S. The heads
